title: Design a Scalable E-commerce Platform
description: Build a system that survives Black Friday spikes. Balance real-time inventory with global content delivery.

steps.detail: Drag an API Server onto the canvas. This handles product listings and searches.

explanation.what: The server responsible for serving product details, categories, and inventory status.
explanation.why: Separating the catalog from the checkout process (microservices) allows you to scale the read-heavy catalog independently of the write-heavy checkout.
explanation.tradeoff: Increased complexity in managing inter-service communication and eventual consistency for inventory counts.
explanation.realWorld: Amazon transitioned from a monolithic 'Obidos' backend to a service-oriented architecture in the early 2000s to solve exactly this.
explanation.capacity: ~200 RPS per instance for typical catalog browse operations.

steps.detail: Drag a CDN and connect it in front of your Catalog.

explanation.what: Caches static assets (images, CSS) and highly-read dynamic content (top-selling product pages) at the network edge.
explanation.why: 90% of e-commerce traffic is 'window shopping' (browsing). A CDN in Tokyo can serve a Japanese user in 10ms instead of 150ms round-trip to a US origin server.
explanation.tradeoff: Cache invalidation lag. If you change a price, users might see the old price for a few minutes until the CDN cache expires.
explanation.realWorld: Shopify uses Fastly and Cloudflare to handle massive spikes during flash sales for major brands.
explanation.capacity: CDNs can absorb 95%+ of catalog traffic, reducing origin load by 20x.

steps.detail: Drag a Cache (Redis) and connect it to your Catalog Server.

explanation.what: In-memory storage for active user sessions and 'Added to Cart' items.
explanation.why: Shopping carts are highly transient. Writing every 'Add to Cart' action to a primary SQL database creates massive write contention. Redis handles this with sub-millisecond latency.
explanation.tradeoff: Data volatility. If Redis crashes without persistence enabled, users lose their carts. (Acceptable for most e-commerce).
explanation.realWorld: eBay uses a massive distributed cache to handle the Billions of cart updates they see daily.
explanation.capacity: Redis handles 100k+ operations per second on a single instance.

questions.question: What is the most critical component for handling a 50x surge in 'browsing' traffic during Black Friday?

explanation.correct: Browsing traffic is read-heavy and static-asset heavy. A CDN offloads the vast majority of this traffic at the edge, preventing it from ever hitting your servers.

wrongAnswers.a: Database replicas help with dynamic reads, but CDN is more efficient for the majority of browsing traffic.
wrongAnswers.c: API servers help but are more expensive and slower to scale than a CDN-hit.
wrongAnswers.d: Load balancers distribute traffic but don't reduce the total work your servers must do.
